Press enter or click to view image in full size

6 min read
Just now

--

In the previous article, we installed Redux Toolkit, understood why both @reduxjs/toolkit and react-redux are required, and organized our project using a professional folder structure.
Now it’s time to build the heart of every Redux application — the Redux Store.
Before writing any code, it’s important to understand one simple fact:
Without a Store, Redux cannot remember or manage your application’s state.
Think of the Store as the brain of your application. Every piece of global data — whether it’s the logged-in user, shopping cart, theme, notifications, or language preference — is stored in one centralized place called the Store.
In this article, we’ll understand what a Store is, how configureStore() creates it, why it replaced the old createStore() method, what middleware does, how Redux DevTools help during development, and how multiple reducers are combined into one Store.
What is the Redux Store?
A Store is the central place where Redux keeps your application’s global state.
Instead of every component maintaining its own copy of shared data, Redux stores that data in one location.
This is why Redux is often described as having a Single Source of Truth.
Imagine you’re building an e-commerce application.
Your application contains:

• Navbar

• Products Page

• Cart Page

• Checkout Page

• Profile Page
The shopping cart should remain the same on every page.
Instead of storing the cart separately inside each component, Redux stores it once inside the Store.
Whenever any component needs the cart, it simply reads it from the Store.
Redux Store
-----------------
| Cart |
| User |
| Theme |
| Notifications |
-----------------
/ | \
Navbar Cart CheckoutThis keeps the application synchronized because everyone reads from the same source.
Why Do We Need a Store?
Imagine every department in a company keeps its own employee records.
Soon you’ll have different versions of the same information.
One department says an employee has resigned.
Another still shows them as active.
A third has an outdated phone number.
Now imagine one centralized HR database.
Everyone reads from the same database.
No duplication.
No inconsistencies.
The Redux Store works exactly the same way.
It ensures that every component always uses the latest state.
Creating a Store Using configureStore()
Redux Toolkit provides a function called configureStore().
Its job is to create the application’s Store.
First, create a folder named app inside the src directory.
src
│
└── app
└── store.jsNow create store.js.
import { configureStore } from "@reduxjs/toolkit";export const store = configureStore({
reducer: {},
});Let’s understand every line.
The first line imports configureStore() from Redux Toolkit.
import { configureStore } from "@reduxjs/toolkit";This function creates a Redux Store with sensible defaults, so you don’t have to configure everything manually.
Next, we create the Store.
export const store = configureStore({
reducer: {},
});The reducer property tells Redux which reducers will manage different parts of the application's state.
Right now it’s empty because we haven’t created any slices yet.
Later, you’ll register reducers like this:
reducer: {
counter: counterReducer,
user: userReducer,
}Here, the Store knows that:

• counterReducer manages the counter state.

• userReducer manages the user state.
Why is it Called configureStore()?
The word configure means to set something up or prepare it.
configureStore() doesn't just create the Store.
It also configures several important features automatically, such as:

• Redux DevTools

• Middleware

• Better error checking

• Recommended defaults

• Development warnings
In other words, it prepares the Store for production-quality applications with almost no extra effort.
Why Did configureStore() Replace createStore()?
Before Redux Toolkit existed, developers created the Store using createStore().
A simple Store looked something like this:
import { createStore } from "redux";const store = createStore(rootReducer);This worked, but real-world applications quickly became complicated.
If you wanted middleware, Redux DevTools, or multiple reducers, you had to configure everything yourself.
A basic Store often grew into dozens of lines of setup code.
Redux Toolkit solved this problem.
Now the same Store can be created much more simply.
import { configureStore } from "@reduxjs/toolkit";const store = configureStore({
reducer: rootReducer,
});Much cleaner.
Much easier.
Much safer.
Today, configureStore() is the officially recommended way to create a Redux Store.
You should avoid using createStore() in new projects because it is considered a legacy API.
What is Middleware?
Middleware is one of the most confusing concepts for beginners, but the idea is actually simple.
Imagine you’re sending a package.
You
↓
Courier Office
↓
DestinationBefore the package reaches its destination, the courier office may:

• Verify the address

• Scan the package

• Record tracking information

• Check for errors
The package still reaches the destination, but extra work happens in between.
Redux Middleware works in exactly the same way.
Dispatch Action
↓
Middleware
↓
Reducer
↓
StoreWhenever an action is dispatched, middleware gets a chance to inspect or modify the process before the reducer updates the state.
Middleware is commonly used for:

• API requests

• Logging

• Authentication

• Analytics

• Error handling

• Async operations
The best part is that Redux Toolkit automatically includes useful middleware, so beginners don’t need to configure it manually.
What are Redux DevTools?
Debugging is one of the biggest challenges in application development.
Imagine trying to find out:

• Which action changed the cart?

• When did the user log in?

• Why did the state suddenly change?
Without debugging tools, this becomes difficult.
Redux DevTools solves this problem.
It records every action that happens inside your application.
For example:
Action HistoryLOGINADD_TO_CARTREMOVE_ITEMCHANGE_THEMELOGOUTYou can click on any action and inspect:

• Previous State

• Current State

• Payload

• Reducer

• Difference between states
It’s like having a complete history of everything that happened inside your application.
The best part?
When using Redux Toolkit, DevTools are automatically enabled during development.
No extra configuration is required.
Combining Multiple Reducers
As your application grows, one reducer won’t be enough.
Imagine your application manages:

• User

• Shopping Cart

• Products

• Theme

• Notifications
Keeping everything inside one reducer would become difficult.
Instead, Redux allows us to split responsibilities.
Example:
userReducerHandles:

• Login

• Logout

• Profile
Another reducer:
cartReducerHandles:

• Add Item

• Remove Item

• Update Quantity
Another:
themeReducerHandles:

• Light Mode

• Dark Mode
Now all of them are registered inside the Store.
import { configureStore } from "@reduxjs/toolkit";import counterReducer from "../features/counter/counterSlice";
import userReducer from "../features/user/userSlice";export const store = configureStore({
reducer: {
counter: counterReducer,
user: userReducer,
},
});Let’s understand this object.
reducer: {
counter: counterReducer,
user: userReducer,
}The key (counter) becomes the name of the state inside the Store.
So your Store will look something like this:
{
counter: {
value: 0
}, user: {
name: "John"
}
}Whenever a component wants the counter value, it reads:
state.counterWhenever it wants user information:
state.userEach reducer is responsible only for its own part of the Store.
This separation keeps applications organized and easier to maintain.
Putting Everything Together
Let’s look at the overall flow.
User Clicks Button
│
▼
dispatch(action)
│
▼
Middleware checks the action
│
▼
Reducer updates the state
│
▼
Store saves the latest state
│
▼
React components automatically re-renderThis predictable flow is one of Redux’s greatest strengths. Every state update follows the same path, making applications easier to understand, debug, and maintain.
Key Takeaways
By now, you should understand the purpose of the Redux Store and why it is the foundation of every Redux Toolkit application.
Here’s what we covered:

• A Store is the centralized location where Redux keeps global application state.

• configureStore() is the modern and recommended way to create a Store.

• It automatically configures useful defaults such as middleware and Redux DevTools.

• configureStore() replaced the older createStore() API because it reduces boilerplate and follows best practices.

• Middleware sits between dispatched actions and reducers, allowing extra work such as logging, API calls, and validation.

• Redux DevTools help developers inspect actions, state changes, and debug applications more effectively.

• Multiple reducers can be combined inside one Store so that each reducer manages only its own piece of state, keeping applications modular and scalable.
What’s Next?
Now that your Redux Store is ready, it’s time to learn about one of the most powerful features of Redux Toolkit: Slices.
In the next article, you’ll learn:

• What a Slice is.

• Why Redux Toolkit introduced createSlice().

• What initialState represents.

• How reducers are written inside a Slice.

• How actions are automatically generated.

• Why writing code that appears to mutate state is actually safe.

• How Immer works behind the scenes to produce immutable updates with simple syntax.
By the end of the next article, you’ll build your first working Slice and understand how Redux Toolkit dramatically simplifies state management compared to traditional Redux.
